IRC §1042 Rollover
Qualified Replacement Securities — CPA Review Guide
Seller
{{sellerName}}
Company
{{companyName}}
Date
{{sentDate}}
Reference
{{ref}}
§ 01What Is §1042?

Internal Revenue Code §1042 allows a seller of C-Corporation stock to an ESOP to defer 100% of the capital gains tax on the sale proceeds, provided the seller reinvests in Qualified Replacement Securities (QRS) within the statutory window.

This memo is prepared for the seller's CPA to review and evaluate applicability. It covers the mechanics, requirements, timing, and limitations of the §1042 election.

Important: Forhemit is not a tax advisor. This document is for informational purposes only and does not constitute tax advice. The seller's CPA should evaluate applicability based on the seller's specific circumstances.
§ 02Eligibility Requirements

To qualify for the §1042 election, all of the following must be true:

A.C-Corporation stock. The seller must sell stock in a C-Corporation to an ESOP. LLCs and S-Corps do not qualify. If the company is currently an LLC or S-Corp, it must convert to a C-Corp before the transaction. The conversion itself has tax implications that the CPA should evaluate.
B.Qualified securities. The stock sold must be stock in a C-Corporation that the seller held for at least 3 years prior to the sale (if acquired after August 10, 1993). Stock acquired by gift, inheritance, or tax-free exchange may have different holding period rules.
C.30% ESOP ownership. Immediately after the sale, the ESOP must own at least 30% of the outstanding stock of the corporation. In a 100% ESOP transaction (like Forhemit's typical deal), this requirement is automatically met.
D.Reinvestment in QRS. The seller must reinvest the sale proceeds in Qualified Replacement Securities within the statutory window (see §03 below).
E.Domestic corporation. The seller must be a domestic (U.S.) taxpayer. Foreign sellers are not eligible.
§ 03Reinvestment Window & QRS Rules
3 months before closing through 12 months after closing
Stock or debt issued by a domestic operating C-Corp
Allowed — election applies proportionally to amounts reinvested

Qualified Replacement Securities include:

1.Stock in any domestic operating C-Corporation. Publicly traded or private. Must be an operating company (not an investment company).
2.Debt instruments of a domestic operating C-Corporation. Bonds, notes, or other debt obligations. Must be issued by an operating company.
3.Stock or debt of certain regulated investment companies. Mutual funds that meet specific requirements under §1042(c)(4). Limited applicability.
Practical note: Most sellers reinvest in a diversified portfolio of publicly traded stocks and bonds through a brokerage account. The seller has 12 months after closing to complete the reinvestment. The 3-month look-back allows pre-positioning, but most sellers reinvest post-closing.
§ 04Tax Mechanics

How the §1042 election works in practice:

A.At closing. The seller receives the full sale proceeds (cash and/or seller note). The capital gain is calculated but not yet recognized for tax purposes.
B.Reinvestment. The seller reinvests the proceeds in QRS within 12 months. The amount reinvested reduces the recognized gain dollar-for-dollar, up to the full amount of the gain.
C.Basis adjustment. The seller's basis in the QRS is reduced by the amount of gain deferred. When the seller eventually sells the QRS, the deferred gain is recognized at that time.
D.Election timing. The §1042 election is made on the seller's federal income tax return for the year of the sale. It is irrevocable once made.
E.Partial election. If the seller reinvests only a portion of the proceeds, the election applies proportionally. The unreinvested portion is taxable in the year of sale.
§ 05Illustrative Comparison

The following comparison illustrates the potential tax savings. These are illustrative only — actual results depend on the seller's specific circumstances, basis, and reinvestment decisions.

Without §1042 election:

{{federalTaxNo1042}}
{{niitTaxNo1042}}
{{stateTaxNo1042}}
{{totalTaxNo1042}}

With §1042 election (100% reinvested in QRS):

$0
$0
{{capitalGain}} (recognized when QRS are sold)
{{totalTaxNo1042}}
§ 06Considerations for the CPA

Key items for CPA review and discussion with the seller:

A.Entity conversion. If the company is currently an LLC or S-Corp, evaluate the tax implications of conversion to C-Corp status before the ESOP transaction. The conversion may trigger gain recognition on appreciated assets.
B.State tax treatment. Not all states conform to §1042. Some states (e.g., California) do not allow the deferral. Verify state-level conformity and calculate the state tax impact.
C.Seller note taxation. If a portion of the sale price is in the form of a seller note, the taxation of installment payments should be analyzed. The §1042 election may apply differently to installment sale proceeds.
D.AMT implications. Evaluate whether the §1042 election affects the seller's alternative minimum tax calculation in the year of sale or in future years.
E.Estate planning. The QRS held by the seller receive a stepped-up basis at death under §1014. This means the deferred gain may never be recognized if the QRS are held until death. Coordinate with estate planning counsel.
F.Net Investment Income Tax (NIIT). The §1042 election defers the NIIT along with the capital gains tax. Confirm that the NIIT applies to the seller's specific situation.
